video_title: Everyone Gave Up On Bitcoin At Exactly The Wrong Time
gist: Jordy Visser (22V Research) argues Bitcoin is bottoming just as retail capitulates: the 'debasement unwind' and inflation-uptick fears are fading, and an 'AI midcycle slowdown' plus rising government involvement in AI recreate the exact conditions Bitcoin was built for. He stays structurally bullish on AI/hardware/memory over 5 years but says the easy beta trade is over and the market's next phase is a broadening-out and a productivity boom, not a bubble bursting.

insights.claim: Bitcoin made fresh lows below both its recent lows and its February lows but held, and combined with bullish technical divergences and heavy ETF selling, Visser now thinks the low is being set and it is worth paying attention to the long side.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A capitulation-plus-divergence setup is a classic risk/reward entry; watch for a reclaim of the 200-day moving average as confirmation.
insights.quote: Finally, we made new lows in Bitcoin, not just from the lows from a couple weeks ago, but also the lows from back in February, and it held in there.

insights.claim: Visser says he will not commit to the long side of Bitcoin until price reclaims the 200-day moving average, which currently sits north of $70,000.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, mechanical trigger level (200-day MA, ~$70k+) to distinguish a bounce from a real trend change.
insights.quote: until it gets above the 200 day, I don't think it's worthwhile for people to to play on the other side.

insights.claim: Retail sentiment toward Bitcoin has fully capitulated, with roughly 60-70% of prior holders questioning their involvement and essentially no new buyers, which Visser frames as the sentiment backdrop of a bottom rather than a top.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Extreme capitulation is contrarian fuel; the absence of marginal sellers is what allows a reversal.
insights.quote: If you've had people that have been involved, at this point, I would say at least 60 to 70% are questioning any involvement they've had.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's 2025 weakness came from three specific headwinds: getting lumped in with the software selloff, Korea rotating capital out of crypto and into memory/leverage trades, and the 'debasement unwind' that dragged it down with gold and silver.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Identifies which headwinds are cyclical and fading (debasement/inflation fears) versus structural, helping time the reversal.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's last leg lower coincided exactly with the market pricing rate hikes back in when the December fed-funds expectation crossed above the ~3.6% effective rate, and Visser believes that repricing has peaked.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Ties Bitcoin's downside directly to rate-hike expectations; a dovish/steady Fed removes the main driver of the selloff.

insights.claim: Visser argues Bitcoin becomes the best-performing major asset once retail can no longer earn 100-1,000% returns in tech beta and is left with mid-teens equity returns, because that low-return environment is what historically feeds Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Frames Bitcoin as an anti-correlation trade to the fading tech-beta bubble; compression in equity returns is the buy signal.
insights.quote: If the best you can get from the S&P 500 is just a guess or then or Q's is 15% a year, well, that's the world that Bitcoin thrives in.

insights.claim: Visser's core call is an 'AI midcycle slowdown': the easy AI beta trade of early 2025 is over and will not return, so future gains require multiple compression and higher volatility rather than a smooth trend higher.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Signals a regime shift from buy-the-dip beta to stock-picking and volatility management in AI names.
insights.quote: the AI midcycle slowdown is basically the peak and the easy money of AI.

insights.claim: Micron topping out despite blowout earnings, and selling off hard after the report, is Visser's tell that AI-infrastructure names are in a consolidation driven by multiple compression, not deteriorating fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Selling off on great news marks a local top; treat strength as an exit rather than a chase in memory names near-term.
insights.quote: when you sell off on great news, that is not a good sign.

insights.claim: Visser expects most AI-infrastructure names to eventually revert to their 50-day moving averages, citing Micron potentially falling from ~$1,200 toward the ~$840 50-day, but expects these stocks to make new all-time highs by Q4 2025.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Gives a specific drawdown roadmap (revert to 50-day, then new highs by Q4) for sizing dip-buys in semis.

insights.claim: Visser insists the slowdown is about multiple compression only, because earnings for the AI-infrastructure companies remain strong and provide an underlying cushion, so there is no AI bubble bursting.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Distinguishes a valuation reset from an earnings collapse; supports buying weakness rather than exiting the theme.
insights.quote: This is about multiple compression.

insights.claim: Visser expects the market to broaden out from hyperscalers into healthcare, insurance and banks, citing early outperformance in those sectors and companies like Travelers and Eli Lilly starting to talk more about AI benefits.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Rotation trade: overweight AI-adopter sectors (health, insurance, financials) over the crowded hyperscaler build-out names.

insights.claim: Visser predicts another ~10% gain in the S&P 500 by year-end driven by broadening rather than hyperscalers, which he thinks can hold the lows but have little upside.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Bullish index target but with leadership shifting away from mega-cap tech; index gains come from the median stock.

insights.claim: Visser reads rising government involvement in AI — model shutdowns, data-center permitting fights, and Sam Altman floating a 5% OpenAI stake to the White House — as recreating the exact fiat/capital-market interference that Bitcoin was designed to escape.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Positions AI-era statism as a fresh structural bull case for Bitcoin as an out-of-system asset.
insights.quote: when the government gets too involved in the capital markets, in the fiat system, in the ability to make money in these things, which is what happens when you shut down the models

insights.claim: Visser frames the AI-government dynamic as politically split, with Anthropic/Dario Amodei on the opposing side and not donors, while OpenAI's Greg Brockman has donated heavily to the Trump campaign and Republican side.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Political alignment could shape which labs get favorable regulation and government contracts.

insights.claim: Visser expects the 2026 midterms to be a net negative for AI, citing anti-AI sentiment rising alongside the electoral success of Mamdani-style candidates.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Political risk premium on AI names into 2026; a catalyst for volatility and for the Bitcoin out-of-system thesis.
insights.quote: I do think the midterms are probably going to be a negative for AI in general

insights.claim: Visser argues AI will provoke capitalists to question what the technology means for the capitalist system and their money, driving major players to seek stores of value outside the system rather than fearing government taxes.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Reframes the biggest long-term Bitcoin catalyst as AI-driven distrust of the capitalist system, not fiscal debasement alone.
insights.quote: I don't think it's the government taxes. I think it's AI.

insights.claim: Visser says compute is structurally short with no near-term solution, and dismisses Meta's pivot to selling data-center compute as a ridiculous cash-flow grab rather than genuine excess capacity, given Meta has been signing deals to secure more capacity.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Read Meta's compute-selling narrative as a bearish tell on its own AI position and a floor-under-the-stock maneuver, not a bullish capacity signal.
insights.quote: There is not enough compute. So, if these guys say they have excess compute when they've done tons of major deals this year to secure more capacity, this honestly is just a way for them to get some cash flow in the door

insights.claim: Visser cites Greg Brockman saying only ~10 million users currently use agentic systems versus a future of billions, as evidence AI adoption is at its very earliest innings regardless of the current selloff.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Massive adoption runway underpins the multi-year bull case for compute and memory even through the midcycle slowdown.
insights.quote: Brockman said right now 10 million users are using Agentic systems. 10 million. We have billions of people that will be using Agentic systems.

insights.claim: Chinese open-source models are only about 6 months behind US frontier models (largely via distillation) and are already better than current US open-source options, creating a real risk that countries cut off from US closed models migrate to Chinese ones.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: US export/regulation restrictions could hand global open-source share to China; watch adoption as a competitive-position indicator.

insights.claim: Visser reframes the Bill Gurley 'Chinese open source always wins' debate: he agrees open source wins, but reads it as small businesses and entrepreneurs winning and the largest-capital players losing, producing a distribution of economics rather than a China victory.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Implies value accrues to AI adopters and SMBs over the mega-cap model owners — supports the broadening-out and out-of-system theses.
insights.quote: It means that small businesses win, that entrepreneurs win, and that pl the places that have the most money don't win.

insights.claim: Visser says he now uses OpenAI's Codex more than Claude because he gets throttled less, attributing OpenAI's edge to having overspent early and secured enough compute while Anthropic plays catch-up.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Compute availability, not just model quality, is becoming the competitive differentiator among frontier labs.
insights.quote: I use Codeex more than Claude at this point. I absolutely do. And the reason is because I'm not throttled as much.

insights.claim: Visser argues memory is the single most important and most durable bottleneck in AI, expects a shortage for a long time, and says watching Micron, SK Hynix and Samsung trade is essential to reading what is happening in AI.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Positions memory names as a core structural long and a real-time barometer for AI demand.
insights.quote: memory is not a bubble. Memory is an insatiable need

insights.claim: Visser tempers memory expectations, saying investors should now expect normal ~30-40% annual returns rather than the 30-40% weekly moves seen recently, which is what is causing the current gyrations.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic return cadence for memory names — still strong, but the parabolic phase is over.
insights.quote: people should expect normal type memory returns of maybe 30 to 40% a year and not 30 to 40% a week.

insights.claim: Visser calls the total AI/robotics buildout a $90 trillion effort and argues investors should never fade the hardware side — memory, infrastructure and physical components — because that is where the spending will flow.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Frames hardware/physical-AI as the durable multi-decade capital-allocation destination.
insights.quote: This is going to be a $90 trillion buildout.

insights.claim: Visser says China is far ahead of the US in robotics production and societal adoption, citing ~140 domestic humanoid companies and a humanoid now released for under $5,000, and frames this as a national-security disadvantage for the US.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Robotics supply chain and adoption become a geopolitical/defense trade; watch Chinese humanoid scaling as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: they have 140 different humanoid robotics companies there and they're now releasing uh, one for less than $5,000.

insights.claim: Visser expects physical AI to appear first in emergency/high-stakes contexts where people won't resist it — drones ahead of police, autonomous ambulances, full self-driving — while in-home humanoids take much longer because they must be near-perfect.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: An adoption-sequencing map for physical AI: emergency/public-safety and FSD lead, consumer humanoids lag.

insights.claim: Visser expects a negative CPI print for June and already for July, driven by collapsing energy and fertilizer prices, and argues the core-inflation-uptick fear that pressured risk assets over the prior 5-6 weeks is fading.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Softer inflation removes the Fed-tightening overhang that drove the Bitcoin and risk-asset selloff.
insights.quote: you're going to get a negative CPI print most likely for the June number. Already for July, we have a negative number.

insights.claim: Visser reads new Fed leadership (Warsh) as wanting lower rates, less forward guidance, and a return to monetary policy as the main lever, while a balance-sheet runoff would be very slow and pre-telegraphed to avoid shocking markets.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A less-hawkish, low-guidance Fed reduces tail risk for risk assets even if it stays on hold.
insights.quote: he wants rates to be lower

insights.claim: Visser says almost no macro strategist actually uses AI daily, so they underestimate its productivity impact; he thinks GDP is mismeasured and that a productivity boom, not a bubble, is what shows up underneath the market.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: If AI productivity is under-measured, consensus underestimates disinflation and earnings power — bullish for equities and eventually Bitcoin.
insights.quote: I very seldom meet any macro person who understands artificial intelligence. And when I say understand it I mean uses it every day for hours a day.

insights.claim: Visser disagrees with Darius Dale on wage inflation, arguing the AI-exposed labor market shows no bounce and that job displacement will worsen with a lag as fired white-collar workers (e.g. accountants) can't quickly retrain into other roles.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A weak labor market keeps the Fed dovish and supports the disinflation/AI-displacement thesis; a specific named disagreement to track.

insights.claim: Visser flags that Q1 earnings were uniquely juiced by two effects — hyperscalers spreading capex expense over five years while chipmakers book revenue immediately, and companies taking mark-ups on stakes in Anthropic, OpenAI and SpaceX — and warns the equity-mark boost runs out after Q2.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Earnings quality is inflated by accounting/mark-to-market effects that fade after Q2 — a reason for the multiple reset.

insights.claim: Visser predicts Anthropic could trade at a $3 trillion valuation when it goes public, drawing an analogy to SpaceX's valuation even if it doesn't hold there.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A potential mega-cap AI IPO catalyst and comparable for private AI marks on public balance sheets.
insights.quote: you could see Anthropic when it goes public. It could trade $3 trillion. There's no reason why it can't.

insights.claim: Visser says the tech-momentum long/short index has run an 85-vol over the last 60 days — higher than any point in 25 years including the dot-com bubble — which forces hedge funds to cut position sizes and makes it structurally harder to stay long tech.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: Record momentum volatility mechanically forces deleveraging; explains rotation pressure and supports lower-vol alternatives.
insights.quote: the tech momentum index I the last 60 days is an 85 V. That's higher than any time over the last 25 years, including the.com bubble.

insights.claim: Visser notes volatility has flipped: Bitcoin now moves only ~1.5% on a big down day (sub-30 vol) while the equity/tech-momentum complex carries the extreme volatility, arguing Bitcoin's relative stability is what lets it lead once money rotates.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: The vol crossover is the mechanism for Visser's rotation-into-Bitcoin call; falling Bitcoin vol makes it institutionally ownable.
insights.quote: now when Bitcoin has a big down day, it's down one and a half percent.

insights.claim: Visser warns of a plausible AI-driven cyber shock within the next year — a hack of a major bank or institution that could take the global market down 10% in a day — and advises investors who can't handle volatility to hold less in the market.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A specific tail-risk catalyst to hedge; reinforces holding uncorrelated assets and sizing for volatility.
insights.quote: there's going to be uh a market shock within the next year where we might come in in a day and the market could be down 10% globally because there was a hacking into a ma a major bank

insights.claim: Visser maintains stocks will outperform bonds for the next couple of years until AGI becomes scary, favors commodities including silver, and holds Bitcoin as the eventual best performer once AI's negatives become apparent.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, 22V Research
insights.so_what: A cross-asset allocation view: long equities and commodities now, with Bitcoin as the asymmetric later-cycle bet.
